- *Paper keywords:* ergodicity, information index, network psychometrics, dynamic exploratory graph analysis, Kolmogorov complexity
- *Additional terms:* DynEGA, GLLA, multiplex networks, intraindividual structure, within-person variability

#### Core Themes
- Introduces the Ergodicity Information Index (EII), quantifying information lost when within-person structures are represented as a single between-person structure
- Uses Kolmogorov complexity theory to compare multiplex (all individual networks) against population network
- Monte Carlo simulation: 216 conditions, 500 replications each
- Three EII methods compared: unweighted (83.5% accuracy), weighted (81.4%), edge list (82.9%)
- Measurement error had the largest impact on accuracy (partial η² = .29–.32)

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Provides a formal tool for testing when population-level personality structures accurately represent individual-level structures — central to the ergodicity question in personality science.

---

### 350. Granziol & Cona (2024)

**Citation:** Granziol, U., & Cona, G. (2024). Architecture and relationships among cognition, mental health and other human domains revealed by network analysis perspective. *Current Psychology, 43*, 4945–4960. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12144-023-04698-8

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional, Factor Analysis (EGA, Bayesian Networks)
**Instruments:** HCP database: NEO-FFI, NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery, ASR, Delay Discounting, AUDIT
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Cognitive Ability, Other (mental health, substance use, pain)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* network analysis, personality, cognitive functions, mental health, individual differences, HCP
- *Additional terms:* Bayesian networks, exploratory graph analysis, crystallized intelligence, delay discounting

#### Core Themes
- EGA on 1,206 HCP participants identified seven domains: Mental Health, Externalizing, High Cognitive Functions, Basic Cognitive Functions, Substance Use, Delay Discounting, Pain
- Crystallized intelligence was the most central variable (highest betweenness) in the entire network
- Cognitive functions clustered into two separate domains, supporting a hierarchical cognitive model
- Bayesian Network revealed HCF→BCF directional influence; Delay Discounting as a "crossroad" between HCF, MTL, and SUB

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Maps network architecture linking personality, cognition, mental health, and behavior in HCP data, showing how different categories of mental phenotypes interrelate structurally.

---

### 351. Henry & Mõttus (2025)

**Citation:** Henry, S., & Mõttus, R. (2025). 100 Nuances of Personality. *European Journal of Personality*. Advance online publication. https://doi.org/10.1177/08902070251314211

**Purpose:** Instrument Validation
**Methodology:** Instrument Validation, Factor Analysis
**Instruments:** 100 Nuances of Personality (100NP; 198 items), SAPA, IPIP, HEXACO-PI-R
**Level of Detail:** Personality Facet, Item-Level

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality assessment, nuances, item pool, test-retest reliability, bottom-up taxonomy
- *Additional terms:* cross-rater agreement, redundancy, bandwidth, efficiency

#### Core Themes
- Develops the 100NP, a 198-item pool maximizing bandwidth, reliability, and non-redundancy at nuance level
- Follows Condon et al. (2020) bottom-up approach; started from 696 items, iteratively reduced through 5 versions
- Selection weights: test-retest (.4), SD (.3), cross-rater agreement (.2), social evaluativeness (.05), clarity (.05)
- 136 items map to Big Five/HEXACO facets; >60 items cover content outside Big Five

#### Results Summary

| Statistic | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Final items | 198 |
| Dev sample N | 858 (self-report), 416 (informant) |
| Test-retest interval | 12 days |
| Median r_tt | .69 (.56–.85) |
| Median r_ca | .37 (.18–.66) |
| Estonian Biobank N | > 77,000 |

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Exemplifies the bottom-up approach to personality measurement, demonstrating that a carefully curated item set can comprehensively assess personality at nuance level with much content lying outside Big Five.

---

### 352. Mulder (2021)

**Citation:** Mulder, R. T. (2021). ICD-11 personality disorders: Utility and implications of the new model. *Frontiers in Psychiatry, 12*, Article 655548. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyt.2021.655548

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review
**Instruments:** ICD-11 PD classification, PiCD, PID-5, DSM-5 AMPD
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Other (personality pathology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality disorder, classification, domains, diagnosis, ICD-11
- *Additional terms:* dimensional model, severity, anankastia, disinhibition, borderline pattern qualifier, DSM-5 AMPD

#### Core Themes
- ICD-11 abolishes all ICD-10 PD categories for a single "personality disorder" diagnosis graded by severity (mild, moderate, severe) plus sub-threshold "personality difficulty"
- Severity is the strongest predictor of suffering and dysfunction
- Five trait domain qualifiers: negative affectivity, detachment, dissociality, anankastia, disinhibition; borderline pattern retained as pragmatic compromise
- ICD-11 trait domains align closely with DSM-5 AMPD; key difference is anankastia (ICD-11) vs. psychoticism (DSM-5)
- Four meta-analyses (52 samples, N = 13,640) confirm personality pathology can be represented as extreme scores on normal personality models

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Exemplifies how psychiatric nosology is shifting from categorical to dimensional trait models grounded in the same structural framework as normal personality.

---

### 353. Menardi et al. (2026)

**Citation:** Menardi, A., Ozer, Z. G., Vallesi, A., Cona, G., & Granziol, U. (2026). Linking individual behavioral architecture to brain topological structure: A combined network analysis approach. *Biological Psychology, 205*, Article 109235. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biopsycho.2026.109235

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional, Factor Analysis (EGA), fMRI connectomics, GAMs
**Instruments:** HCP behavioral battery, NEO-FFI, resting-state fMRI (Schaefer atlas, 7 RSNs)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Cognitive Ability, Other (brain network topology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* brain network topology, graph theory, resting-state fMRI, cognitive-behavioral domains
- *Additional terms:* connectome, FPN, DMN, participation coefficient, clustering coefficient, segregation, integration

#### Core Themes
- Used EGA on HCP behavioral measures to derive cognitive-behavioral domains, then related to resting-state brain topology via graph-theoretic metrics
- Efficient cognition associated with more segregated DMN but more integrated FPN and VAN
- Maladaptive domains (impulsivity, substance use) showed opposite pattern: greater DMN-SMN integration, DAN segregation
- Brain-behavior effect sizes generally small (rho ~0.1), consistent with large-sample literature

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Demonstrates how brain network architecture maps onto cognitive and behavioral phenotypes, illustrating neural infrastructure underlying individual differences.

---

### 354. Michelini et al. (2024)

**Citation:** Michelini, G., Carlisi, C. O., Eaton, N. R., Elison, J. T., Haltigan, J. D., Kotov, R., Krueger, R. F., Latzman, R. D., Li, J. J., Levin-Aspenson, H. F., Salum, G. A., South, S. C., Stanton, K., Waldman, I. D., & Wilson, S. (2024). Where do neurodevelopmental conditions fit in transdiagnostic psychiatric frameworks? Incorporating a new neurodevelopmental spectrum. *World Psychiatry, 23*(3), 333–357. https://doi.org/10.1002/wps.21225

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review, Systematic Review
**Instruments:** References CBCL, ASEBA, PID-5, SRS, ASRS, various neurodevelopmental measures
**Level of Detail:** Other (transdiagnostic spectra)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* neurodevelopmental spectrum, ADHD, autism, transdiagnostic, HiTOP, dimensional, comorbidity
- *Additional terms:* learning disorders, intellectual disabilities, externalizing, internalizing, RDoC

#### Core Themes
- Proposes a transdiagnostic neurodevelopmental spectrum (ADHD, ASD, learning, intellectual, communication, motor disorders) within hierarchical models like HiTOP
- Reviews evidence across genetics/heritability, environmental risk, developmental course, temperament, cognition, neurobiology, and clinical utility
- Table 2 provides comprehensive evidence map across ~30 validators for 6 neurodevelopmental conditions
- Dimensional measures show higher reliability and temporal stability than categorical diagnoses
- Three-cohort study (N = 1,253): neurodevelopmental factor predicted functioning ~2× more strongly than DSM diagnoses

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Directly addresses how a broad class of mental phenotypes can be reconceptualized as a transdiagnostic spectrum with shared developmental origins, genetic architecture, and neural substrates.

---

### 355. Michelini et al. (2025)

**Citation:** Michelini, G., Liao, W., Lu, S., Caserini, C., Eley, T. C., Ronald, A., Wilson, S., Malanchini, M., & Rimfeld, K. (2025). The neurodevelopmental spectrum: Phenotypic architecture, etiology, and predictive utility across development. *medRxiv*. https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.07.31.25332492

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Longitudinal, Twin/Genetic, Factor Analysis (ESEM)
**Instruments:** TEDS battery (75–180 items/wave), polygenic scores, cognitive measures (g, reading, exams)
**Level of Detail:** Other (neurodevelopmental spectrum factor), Cognitive Ability, g

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* neurodevelopmental spectrum, ADHD, autism, TEDS, twin study, heritability, polygenic scores
- *Additional terms:* ESEM, internalizing, externalizing, Goldberg's bass-ackwards, developmental trajectory

#### Core Themes
- First comprehensive empirical investigation of the neurodevelopmental spectrum across childhood and adolescence using TEDS longitudinal twin data (N ~7,000–8,000)
- ESEM identified a neurodevelopmental factor at ages 7, 12, 16, capturing ADHD and autism features plus learning/motor difficulties in childhood
- Twin analyses revealed increasing heritability: h² = .60 (age 7), .79 (age 12), .82 (age 16)
- Moderate-to-large genetic overlap across ages (r_g: .45–.70)
- Polygenic scores for neurodevelopmental conditions explained up to 3.36–3.93% of variance combined
- The factor predicted cognitive/educational outcomes (R² up to 20.61% cross-sectionally)

#### Results Summary

| Parameter | Age 7 | Age 12 | Age 16 |
|---|---|---|---|
| h² | .60 | .79 | .82 |
| e² | .40 | .16 | .18 |
| r_g with next wave | .45 | .70 | — |

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Most comprehensive empirical evidence for a neurodevelopmental spectrum phenotype, demonstrating structural replicability, strong genetic basis with increasing heritability, and predictive utility for real-world outcomes.

---

### 356. Ringwald et al. (2022)

**Citation:** Ringwald, W. R., Manuck, S. B., Marsland, A. L., & Wright, A. G. C. (2022). Psychometric evaluation of a Big Five personality state scale for intensive longitudinal studies. *Assessment, 29*(6), 1301–1319. https://doi.org/10.1177/10731911211008254

**Purpose:** Instrument Validation
**Methodology:** Experience Sampling, Instrument Validation, Factor Analysis (MEFA)
**Instruments:** 20-item and 10-item Big Five state scales, BFI-2, NEO-PI-R, PANAS
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality assessment, personality states, intensive longitudinal design, multilevel modeling
- *Additional terms:* between-person variance, within-person variance, ICC, omega reliability, nomological net

#### Core Themes
- First systematic psychometric evaluation of Big Five personality state scales at both between- and within-person levels across three independent samples
- MEFA (pooled N = 1,041, 7,664 days) confirmed five-factor structure at both levels: CFI = .96, RMSEA = .03
- Between-person ω = .86; within-person ω = .51 — within-person reliability notably lower
- Average ICC = .61 (61% between-persons, 39% within-person fluctuation)
- ~64% of 220 nomological associations replicated across all three samples

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Demonstrates that personality phenotypes can be validly measured at both stable trait and fluctuating state levels, illustrating how dimensional structure operates at different timescales.

---

### 357. Ringwald et al. (2023a)

**Citation:** Ringwald, W. R., Emery, L., Khoo, S., Clark, L. A., Kotelnikova, Y., Scalco, M. D., Watson, D., Wright, A. G. C., & Simms, L. J. (2023). Structure of pathological personality traits through the lens of the CAT-PD model. *Assessment, 30*(7), 2276–2295. https://doi.org/10.1177/10731911221143343

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional, Factor Analysis (EFA, ESEM), Measurement Invariance
**Instruments:** CAT-PD (33 trait scales), PID-5, BFI-2, NEO-PI-3, IPIP-NEO-120, FI-FFM, IIP-SC
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality disorders, transdiagnostic, assessment, dimensional trait models, maladaptive traits
- *Additional terms:* CAT-PD, Anankastia, Disinhibition, Psychoticism, ICD-11, DSM-5 Section III, target rotation

#### Core Themes
- First standalone structural evaluation of CAT-PD domain structure in large pooled sample (N = 3,987, 8 subsamples, 352 clinical + 3,635 non-clinical)
- Both 5- and 6-factor models fit acceptably; 6-factor separates Anankastia from Disinhibition
- Measurement invariance established across gender, sample type, and race
- CAT-PD domains showed good convergent validity with PID-5 (r = .63–.78) and lower intercorrelations (median r = .30 vs. PID-5 median r = .51)
- Self-Harm loaded on Psychoticism; Cognitive Problems and Fantasy Proneness loaded on Disinhibition

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Establishes the empirical structure of a comprehensive pathological personality measure, clarifying whether five or six domains best describe the maladaptive personality space.

---

### 358. Ringwald, Forbes & Wright (2023)

**Citation:** Ringwald, W. R., Forbes, M. K., & Wright, A. G. C. (2023). Meta-analysis of structural evidence for the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP) model. *Psychological Medicine, 53*(2), 533–546. https://doi.org/10.1017/S0033291721001902

**Purpose:** Meta-Analysis
**Methodology:** Meta-Analysis, Factor Analysis
**Instruments:** Structured/semi-structured diagnostic interviews (23 DSM diagnoses)
**Level of Detail:** Other (DSM diagnoses, psychopathology spectra)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* HiTOP, meta-analysis, factor analysis, transdiagnostic, psychopathology structure
- *Additional terms:* internalizing, externalizing, thought disorder, detachment, antagonism, disinhibition, general factor p

#### Core Themes
- First quantitative meta-analysis providing direct empirical support for HiTOP (35 studies, 48 samples, N = 120,596)
- Five-factor model: CFI = .92, RMSEA = .07, SRMR = .03
- Factors: Internalizing, Detachment, Thought Disorder, Antagonism, Disinhibition
- Hierarchical: general p → Externalizing/Internalizing → Psychosis splits → Antagonism/Disinhibition splits → Thought Disorder/Detachment
- Personality disorders (borderline, paranoid, schizotypal) were strongest markers of general factor p

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
First quantitative structural validation of the dominant dimensional model for organizing psychopathology, with meta-analytic loadings quantifying how strongly each DSM diagnosis reflects higher-order dimensions.

---

### 359. Ringwald et al. (2025a)

**Citation:** Ringwald, W. R., Abramovitch, A., Agelink van Rentergem, J. A., & Kotov, R. (2025). Do cognitive functions belong in the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology (HiTOP) model? A meta-analysis. *Perspectives on Psychological Science, 20*(6), 1049–1064. https://doi.org/10.1177/17456916251347926

**Purpose:** Meta-Analysis
**Methodology:** Meta-Analysis, Factor Analysis
**Instruments:** Neuropsychological tests (7 cognitive domains), structured diagnostic interviews (8 DSM disorders)
**Level of Detail:** Cognitive Ability, Other (psychopathology superspectra)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* cognitive functions, HiTOP, psychopathology structure, meta-analysis, neuropsychological tests
- *Additional terms:* joint factor model, psychoticism superspectrum, cognitive dysfunction, RDoC

#### Core Themes
- First meta-analytic joint factor analysis integrating cognitive abilities and psychopathology (80+ studies, 63 meta-analyses, N > 300,000)
- Critical finding: cognitive functions do NOT form a separate domain — a separate cognitive factor never emerged even with up to 8 factors
- One-factor: all disorders and cognitive functions loaded onto general factor (median λ = |.51|)
- Three-factor: Psychoticism + Cognitive Dysfunction, Externalizing, Emotional Dysfunction
- Cognitive functions co-loaded with psychotic disorders, providing the most direct evidence for cognition's place in the psychoticism superspectrum

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Demonstrates that cognitive abilities are integral to psychopathology structure — not a separate domain — fundamentally expanding our understanding of what mental phenotypes encompass.

---

### 360. Ringwald (2025)

**Citation:** Ringwald, W. R. (2025). A call to action for explaining rather than merely predicting psychopathology. Unpublished manuscript, Department of Psychology, University of Minnesota.

**Purpose:** Commentary/Editorial
**Methodology:** Narrative Review
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Other (psychopathology, mechanistic traits)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* psychopathology, mechanistic traits, prediction vs. explanation, dimensional models
- *Additional terms:* HiTOP, person-environment transactions, intensive longitudinal methods, personalized treatment

#### Core Themes
- Argues psychopathology research has overemphasized prediction (who is at risk) at the expense of explanation (what mechanisms maintain dysfunction)
- Distinguishes demographic/social predictors (race, SES) that identify risk groups from mechanistic psychological traits that causally maintain psychopathology
- Advocates studying modifiable, causally proximal traits assessable via intensive longitudinal methods
- Links to person-environment transactions framework and cybernetic theory of psychopathology

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Directly addresses how mental phenotypes should be understood through mechanistic trait processes rather than categorical prediction, aligning with the book's emphasis on understanding how psychological structure is generated and maintained.

---

### 361. Russell-Lasalandra & Golino (2026)

**Citation:** Russell-Lasalandra, L. L., & Golino, H. (2026). Prompt engineering for scale development in generative psychometrics. *arXiv preprint*. arXiv:2603.15909. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2603.15909

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Experimental/Intervention (simulation), Factor Analysis (EGA)
**Instruments:** AI-GENIE pipeline, GPT-4o, GPT-5.1, GPT-OSS-120B, GPT-OSS-20B
**Level of Detail:** Item-Level, Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* prompt engineering, adaptive prompting, LLM-generated items, psychometric scale development
- *Additional terms:* AI-GENIE, NMI, item redundancy, structural validity, few-shot learning, persona prompting

#### Core Themes
- Tests six prompt engineering strategies interacting with model capability and temperature for LLM-generated psychometric items
- Adaptive prompting (feeding back prior items) emerges as the dominant quality driver
- Benefits scale with model capability: GPT-5.1 showed ~93% redundancy reduction under adaptive prompting
- Non-adaptive strategies (expanded, persona, few-shot) produced minimal improvements over basic prompt
- Post-reduction, adaptive prompting retained ~2× as many items as basic prompting with higher NMI

#### Results Summary

| Model + Strategy | Redundant items removed | Final NMI |
|---|---|---|
| GPT-5.1 (Adaptive) | ~2–3 | ~97–98% |
| GPT-5.1 (Basic) | ~35.9 | ~87–92% |
| GPT-4o (Adaptive) | ~2.7 | ~90–93% |

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Demonstrates how AI tools can be disciplined by psychometric methods to generate structurally valid personality items — relevant to how measurement instruments for mental phenotypes can be constructed.

---

### 362. Samo et al. (2026)

**Citation:** Samo, A., Garrido, L. E., Abad, F. J., Golino, H., McAbee, S. T., & Christensen, A. P. (2026). Revisiting the IPIP-NEO personality hierarchy with taxonomic graph analysis. *European Journal of Personality, 40*(2), 369–390. https://doi.org/10.1177/08902070251352590

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis (Taxonomic Graph Analysis), Item-Level
**Instruments:** IPIP-NEO-300 (N = 149,337)
**Level of Detail:** Item-Level, Personality Facet, Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality taxonomy, network analysis, hierarchical, exploratory graph analysis
- *Additional terms:* TGA, Stability, Plasticity, Disinhibition, Sociability, Integrity, Impulsivity, bottom-up structure

#### Core Themes
- Introduces TGA, a 7-step network psychometrics framework for bottom-up hierarchical structure recovery
- Applied to 300-item IPIP-NEO (N = 149,337): 3-level hierarchy — 28 facets, 6 traits, 3 meta-traits
- Only 16/30 theoretical facets emerged empirically (53.3%)
- Second level: Neuroticism, Conscientiousness, Openness, Sociability (E+A mix), Integrity (A+C morality), Impulsivity
- Third level: Stability, Plasticity, Disinhibition (novel meta-trait); no general factor supported (u = 0.49)
- Sociability combined Extraversion facets (Cheerfulness, Gregariousness) with Agreeableness facets (Empathy, Trust)

#### Results Summary

| Level | Dimensions |
|---|---|
| First (facets) | 28 (from 221 items) |
| Second (traits) | 6: N, C, O, Sociability, Integrity, Impulsivity |
| Third (meta-traits) | 3: Stability, Plasticity, Disinhibition |
| Fourth | Not supported (u = 0.49) |

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Rigorous bottom-up empirical map of personality structure at item level, revealing that the phenotypic architecture departs substantially from theoretical Big Five — with novel Disinhibition meta-trait and Sociability/Integrity as alternatives to E and A.

---

### 363. Thalmayer et al. (2025)

**Citation:** Thalmayer, A. G., Mather, K. A., Saucier, G., Naude, L., Florence, M., Adonis, T.-A., Shino, E. N., Asatsa, S., Witzlack-Makarevich, A., Bachlin, L. Z. M., & Condon, D. M. (2025). The cross-cultural big two: A culturally decentered theoretical and measurement model for personality traits. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 128*(5), 1181–1208. https://doi.org/10.1037/pspp0000528

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional, Factor Analysis, Instrument Validation, Measurement Invariance
**Instruments:** CCB2I (45 items), IPIP IPC, QB6, SPI-27, IMHA, Duke Religion Index
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain (Big Two)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* cross-cultural assessment, big two, agency and communion, decolonization of psychology
- *Additional terms:* Social Self-Regulation, Dynamism, culturally decentered, ALLS, World Englishes, interpersonal circumplex

#### Core Themes
- Develops a "big two" model and CCB2I from global lexical evidence rather than Western-originated frameworks
- Communion/Social Self-Regulation: warmth, morality, respect, industriousness, even temper
- Agency/Dynamism: competence, confidence, fearlessness, positive mood, sociability
- Study 1: 55 recurrent terms from two-factor solutions across 11 lexical studies
- Study 2: Refined in ALLS (N = 2,496 African 18-year-olds) and SAPA (N = 61,760, 13 countries)
- Communion/SSR predicted lower Externalizing; Agency/Dynamism predicted lower Internalizing

#### Results Summary

| Scale | ALLS α | SAPA α | SAPA ω_h | r (between) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Communion/SSR | .90–.91 | .87 | .63 | .33–.53 |
| Agency/Dynamism | .78–.87 | .91 | .57 | |

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Addresses cross-cultural universality of personality structure by proposing a two-factor model from global lexical evidence, central to understanding how broad personality phenotypes are culturally decentered.

---

### 364. Vicentin et al. (2026)

**Citation:** Vicentin, S., Granziol, U., Romano, D., Scandola, M., & Cona, G. (2026). The effect of age on the architecture of psychological and cognitive dimensions: A network perspective. *European Journal of Ageing, 23*, 10. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10433-025-00903-8

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional, Factor Analysis (EGA, Network Comparison Test)
**Instruments:** NEO-FFI, Penn Emotion Recognition Task, Delay Discounting, NIH Toolbox Battery, ASR/OASR
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Cognitive Ability, Other (mental health)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Human Connectome Project, network, ageing, exploratory graph analysis, network comparison test
- *Additional terms:* dedifferentiation hypothesis, socioemotional selectivity, cognitive-personality interplay

#### Core Themes
- HCP-YA and HCP-Aging data: 3 age groups (Young n = 230, Middle n = 242, Older n = 209)
- Young Adults: 4 communities (Low/High Cognition, Mental Health, Externalizing)
- Middle-aged and Older Adults: cognitive variables merge into single community (dedifferentiation)
- Emotion recognition and delay discounting migrate from Cognition to Mental Health community in older adults
- Global network strength decreases with age (8.27 → 5.21, p = .016)
- Openness consistently clusters with Cognition across all ages

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Demonstrates how the architecture linking personality, cognition, and psychopathology reorganizes across adulthood, supporting dedifferentiation and showing that mental phenotypes dynamically reconfigure with development.

---

### 365. Vu et al. (2026)

**Citation:** Vu, H., Nguyen, H. A., Ganesan, A. V., Juhng, S., Kjell, O. N. E., Sedoc, J., Kern, M. L., Boyd, R. L., Ungar, L., Schwartz, H. A., & Eichstaedt, J. C. (2026). PsychAdapter: Adapting LLMs to reflect traits, personality, and mental health. *npj Artificial Intelligence, 2*, 26. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44387-026-00071-9

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Experimental/Intervention, Instrument Validation
**Instruments:** PsychAdapter on Gemma-2B, GPT-2 Large, LLaMA-3-8B; Big Five (text-estimated); IPC
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Other (depression, life satisfaction)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* PsychAdapter, differential language analysis, personality-conditioned text generation
- *Additional terms:* transformer architecture, fine-grained personality control, computational personality assessment

#### Core Themes
- PsychAdapter adds <0.1% parameters to auto-regressive transformers, enabling text generation conditioned on continuous personality/mental health scores
- Trained on blog posts (19,320 authors) and tweets (1 billion anonymized)
- Human expert evaluation: 87.3% accuracy identifying Big Five levels; 96.7% for mental health
- Claude 3.5 Sonnet as automated annotator: 93.5% Big Five accuracy, 100% mental health
- Generalizes across text domains, LLM architectures, and psychological constructs

#### Results Summary

| Evaluator | Big Five Accuracy (3-level) | Mental Health Accuracy |
|---|---|---|
| Human raters | 87.3% | 96.7% |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 93.5% | 100% |
| Inter-rater κ | 0.76 | 1.00 |

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Demonstrates that personality phenotypes are reflected in language at sufficient granularity for LLMs to generate trait-reflective text, relevant to the language-personality interface.

---

### 366. Wright et al. (2026)

**Citation:** Wright, A. G. C., Ringwald, W. R., Vize, C. E., Eichstaedt, J. C., Schwartz, H. A., Taxali, A., Angstadt, M., & Sripada, C. (2026). Assessing personality using zero-shot generative AI scoring of brief open-ended text. *Nature Human Behaviour, 10*(3), 541–555. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-025-02389-x

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional, Experimental/Intervention
**Instruments:** NEO-FFI, IPIP-NEO-120, CAT-PD, IIP-SC, SCID-5, SIDP-IV, 7 LLMs
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Item-Level

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* zero-shot personality assessment, LLM scoring, open-ended text, Big Five
- *Additional terms:* convergent validity, nomological network, ecological momentary assessment, stream of consciousness, video diaries, generative AI

#### Core Themes
- Tests whether 7 commercial LLMs can score Big Five from unconstrained qualitative text without task-specific training
- Sample 1: 60 participants, 30-min streams of thought + NEO-FFI
- Sample 2: 108 participants, nightly video diaries (15–21 days) + IPIP-NEO-120 + extensive external measures
- Convergent correlations (LLM average vs. self-report): r = .42–.48, comparable to self-other agreement benchmarks
- Nomological network profile correlations: r_s = .62–.96
- LLMs consistently outperformed LIWC and generally matched/exceeded MyPersonality ML model
- Average across 7 LLMs gave strongest convergence ("wisdom of crowds")

#### Results Summary

| Trait | Sample 1 r | Sample 2 r | Nomological profile r |
|---|---|---|---|
| Extraversion | ~.46 | ~.57 | .96 |
| Neuroticism | ~.44 | ~.49 | .92 |
| Openness | ~.53 | varies | .78 |
| Conscientiousness | ~.46 | ~.26 | .72 |
| Agreeableness | ~.46 | ~.28 | .62 |
| LLM Average | .48 | .44 | — |

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Demonstrates that personality phenotypes are robustly embedded in natural language — off-the-shelf AI can extract reliable Big Five scores from brief unconstrained narratives, bridging the psychometric and lexical traditions through computational methods.

---

### 367. Ashton & Lee (2016)

**Citation:** Ashton, M. C., & Lee, K. (2016). Age trends in HEXACO-PI-R self-reports. *Journal of Research in Personality, 64*, 102–111. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jrp.2016.08.008

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional
**Instruments:** HEXACO-PI-R
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* HEXACO, age trends, personality development, facets
- *Additional terms:* Honesty-Humility, Emotionality, cross-sectional age differences, cohort effects

#### Core Themes
- Facet-level age trends in ~100,000 participants (ages 14–74) using the HEXACO-PI-R
- Facet-level trends diverge notably from domain-level trends, particularly within Emotionality and Extraversion
- Provides HEXACO-specific developmental data that does not map 1:1 onto NEO/Big Five age trajectories

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Largest cross-sectional HEXACO age-trend dataset; complements Big Five developmental work but facets don't map directly onto NEO-PI-R.

### 368. Ma et al. (2025)

**Citation:** Ma, W., Nissen, A., Graham, E., Hawks, Z., & Beck, E. (2025). Personality states and cognitive ability and variability in older adults. *Innovation in Aging, 9*(Suppl. 2), 84.

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Experience Sampling
**Instruments:** Big Five (EMA states)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Cognitive Ability

- *Additional terms:* EMA, within-person variability, Big Five states, cognition

#### Core Themes
- EMA study (N ≈ 200, ages 45–74) examining personality state–cognition associations
- Published conference abstract related to the full preprint (Ma et al., under review)

Conference abstract only (1 page); insufficient detail for substantive analysis. See Ma et al. (under review) for the full study.

### 369. Seifert, Rohrer, & Schmukle (in press)

**Citation:** Seifert, I. S., Rohrer, J. M., & Schmukle, S. C. (in press). Using within-person change in three large panel studies to estimate personality age trajectories. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology*. https://doi.org/10.1037/pspp0000482

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Longitudinal, Fixed Effects Modeling
**Instruments:** Panel survey items (SOEP and other large panels)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Five, personality development, within-person change, fixed effects, panel studies
- *Additional terms:* age trajectories, SOEP, domain-level, item-level, maturity principle

#### Core Themes
- Gold-standard within-person design (fixed effects) eliminates all stable between-person confounds across three independent panels (combined N = 48,264)
- Three independent samples replicate, providing strong evidence for within-person age trajectories
- Domain- and item-level only — no facet-level analysis; short instruments (3–10 items per trait)

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Strongest methodological design for personality age trajectories — separates within-person change from between-person differences. Complements Seifert et al. (2023) which uses similar methods but different author team and panels.

---

### 370. Almagor, Tellegen, & Waller (1995)

**Citation:** Almagor, M., Tellegen, A., & Waller, N. G. (1995). The Big Seven Model: A cross-cultural replication and further exploration of the basic dimensions of natural language trait descriptors. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 69*(2), 300–307. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.69.2.300

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis, Cross-Cultural (Hebrew lexical study)
**Instruments:** 252 Hebrew trait descriptors
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Seven, personality structure, lexical approach, cross-cultural, Hebrew
- *Additional terms:* Positive Valence, Negative Valence, evaluative dimensions, Big Five comparison

#### Core Themes
- Hebrew lexical study using 252 trait descriptors rated by 637 participants; largely replicates the Big Seven model
- Two evaluative factors — Positive Valence and Negative Valence — emerge prominently, not represented in the Big Five
- Factor VII yielded a communal (vs. agentic) Positive Emotionality split rather than the expected Conventionality dimension

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Early cross-cultural evidence that personality structure extends beyond the Big Five when evaluative and state descriptors are included in the lexicon.

---

### 371. Ashton, Lee, & Goldberg (2004)

**Citation:** Ashton, M. C., Lee, K., & Goldberg, L. R. (2004). A hierarchical analysis of 1,710 English personality-descriptive adjectives. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 87*(5), 707–721. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.87.5.707

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis (hierarchical, 1–7 factor solutions)
**Instruments:** 1,710 English personality-descriptive adjectives (Goldberg, 1982 compilation)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality structure, lexical approach, English, HEXACO, Honesty-Humility
- *Additional terms:* hierarchical factors, six-factor model, Religiosity, Intellect–Imagination, Unconventionality

#### Core Themes
- Six-factor solution produces an Honesty-Humility factor (unpretentious vs. sly) in English for the first time, paralleling findings in seven other languages
- Five-factor solution resembles Big Five but includes rotational variants of Agreeableness and Emotional Stability similar to cross-linguistic patterns
- Hierarchical emergence explored from 1 to 7 factors; 7th factor = Religiosity

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Seminal study establishing that Honesty-Humility exists in the English lexicon and providing the empirical basis for the HEXACO model.

---

### 372. Benet & Waller (1995)

**Citation:** Benet, V., & Waller, N. G. (1995). The Big Seven factor model of personality description: Evidence for its cross-cultural generality in a Spanish sample. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 69*(4), 701–718. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.69.4.701

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis, Cross-Cultural (US + Spain)
**Instruments:** Big Seven questionnaire (Tellegen, Grove & Waller, 1991), BFI
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Seven, personality structure, cross-cultural, Spanish, evaluative dimensions
- *Additional terms:* Positive Valence, Negative Valence, Positive Emotionality, self- and peer-ratings

#### Core Themes
- Big Seven factor structure replicates in Spanish across self- and peer-ratings (US N=569, Spain N=435)
- Positive and Negative Valence factors emerge reliably in nonclinical samples from both cultures
- Culturally meaningful item-level differences: Spaniards appear to evaluate introversion and unconventionality differently

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Cross-cultural evidence for evaluative personality dimensions beyond the Big Five in a Romance-language culture.

---

### 373. Costa & McCrae (1995)

**Citation:** Costa, P. T., Jr., & McCrae, R. R. (1995). Domains and facets: Hierarchical personality assessment using the Revised NEO Personality Inventory. *Journal of Personality Assessment, 64*(1), 21–50. https://doi.org/10.1207/s15327752jpa6401_2

**Purpose:** Review / Instrument Validation
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis, Facet Analysis, Profile Interpretation
**Instruments:** NEO-PI-R
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* five-factor model, NEO-PI-R, facets, hierarchical assessment, personality assessment
- *Additional terms:* discriminant validity, facet analysis, profile interpretation, domain-and-facet approach

#### Core Themes
- Describes the conceptual logic behind the NEO-PI-R's 5 domain × 6 facet structure and evidence for discriminant validity of the 30 facet scales
- Introduces "facet analysis" — interpreting external scales by their correlations with specific NEO-PI-R facets
- Advocates hierarchical interpretation: domain-level for rapid overview, facet-level for detailed assessment

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Foundational description of the domain-and-facet approach that is central to the facet-level analysis framework of the project.

---

### 374. De Raad, Barelds, et al. (2014)

**Citation:** De Raad, B., Barelds, D. P. H., Timmerman, M. E., De Roover, K., Mlačić, B., & Church, A. T. (2014). Towards a pan-cultural personality structure: Input from 11 psycholexical studies. *European Journal of Personality, 28*(5), 497–510. https://doi.org/10.1002/per.1953

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Simultaneous Component Analysis, Cross-Cultural
**Instruments:** Trait terms from 11 psycholexical taxonomies (English, Dutch, German, Italian, Hungarian, Polish, Czech, Filipino, Greek, Croatian)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* lexical studies, cross-cultural, simultaneous component analysis, pan-cultural structure
- *Additional terms:* Dynamism, Affiliation, Order, three-factor kernel, six-factor model

#### Core Themes
- Joint analysis of 11 lexical studies (total N=7,104, 1,993 trait terms translated to English): three-component kernel is most robust
- Three dimensions: Dynamism (≈ Extraversion), Affiliation (≈ Agreeableness), Order (≈ Conscientiousness)
- Six-component solution aligns with HEXACO but replicability decreases beyond three factors

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Provides the strongest empirical case for which personality dimensions are genuinely pan-cultural vs. culture-specific; three-factor kernel challenges both Big Five and HEXACO universality claims.

---

### 375. Fetvadjiev & van de Vijver (2015)

**Citation:** Fetvadjiev, V. H., & van de Vijver, F. J. R. (2015). Universality of the five-factor model of personality. In J. D. Wright (Ed.), *International Encyclopedia of the Social & Behavioral Sciences* (2nd ed., Vol. 9, pp. 249–253). Elsevier. https://doi.org/10.1016/B978-0-08-097086-8.25067-8

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Five, FFM, culture, universality, psycholexical, indigenous psychology
- *Additional terms:* emic–etic approach, Openness replicability, social-relational dimensions

#### Core Themes
- Reviews three approaches to FFM universality: questionnaire studies, lexical studies, and indigenous/emic–etic studies
- Four factors replicate well across cultures (N, E, A, C); Openness is weakest
- Lexical perspective supports only 2–3 dimensions as truly universal; social-relational constructs may extend FFM in some cultures

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Concise encyclopedia overview of the state of evidence on Big Five universality, useful as a summary reference.

---

### 376. Mai & Church (2023)

**Citation:** Mai, N. T. Q., & Church, A. T. (2023). Exploring the indigenous structure of Vietnamese personality traits: A psycho-lexical approach. *International Journal of Personality Psychology, 9*, 1–26. https://doi.org/10.21827/ijpp.9.41003

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis (PCA with varimax), Cross-Cultural
**Instruments:** 668 Vietnamese trait terms (reduced from 2,129)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality structure, Vietnamese, psycholexical, indigenous, cross-cultural
- *Additional terms:* Big One, Big Two, Big Three, Big Six, ML7, Warmheartedness-Virtue

#### Core Themes
- First Vietnamese psycholexical study (N=710): Big One, Big Two, and Big Three replicate moderately to strongly
- Big Five, Big Six (HEXACO), and ML7 do not replicate well in Vietnamese
- Eight-factor solution is most interpretable, with five factors aligning with Big Six dimensions plus three indigenous factors

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Important non-Indo-European lexical study showing the limits of Big Five/Six replicability in Southeast Asia and supporting the three-factor cross-cultural kernel.

---

### 377. Peabody & De Raad (2002)

**Citation:** Peabody, D., & De Raad, B. (2002). The substantive nature of psycholexical personality factors: A comparison across languages. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 83*(4), 983–997. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.83.4.983

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Content Analysis of Factor Structures, Cross-Cultural
**Instruments:** Trait terms from 6 European lexical studies (Triestean, Hungarian, Roman Italian, Dutch, Polish, Czech)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Five, psycholexical, cross-cultural, factor content, personality structure
- *Additional terms:* Assertiveness, Helpfulness, Forcefulness, Conceitedness, content categories, factor splitting

#### Core Themes
- Substantive content analysis of factors across 6 languages using a taxonomy of content categories
- Conscientiousness (Factor III) generalizes best across all languages
- Emotional Stability (IV) and Intellect/Openness (V) generally do not cohere; Extraversion (I) and Agreeableness (II) tend to split

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Key challenge to Big Five universality at the content level — shows which trait categories replicate vs. fracture across languages.

---

### 378. Tellegen (2018)

**Citation:** Tellegen, A. (2018). *The Lexical Big Seven: An Inclusive Psycholexical Investigation*. Unpublished manuscript.

**Purpose:** Original Research / Monograph
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis (5 studies), Replication
**Instruments:** S400 (400-item self-report questionnaire), S60 (60-item replication inventory)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Seven, lexical hypothesis, personality structure, evaluative descriptors
- *Additional terms:* Positive Valence, Negative Valence, Positive Emotionality, Negative Emotionality, Disagreement Proneness, Unconventionality, Dependability

#### Core Themes
- Definitive statement of the Big Seven by its originator: comprehensive psycholexical investigation using inclusive sampling (not excluding evaluative or state terms)
- Seven dimensions: Negative Valence, Positive Emotionality, Positive Valence, Negative Emotionality, Disagreement Proneness, Unconventionality, Dependability
- Five studies address structural robustness, role of familiarity, significance of evaluative descriptors, and replication

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
The authoritative source for the Big Seven model; essential for understanding what is gained (evaluative dimensions, clinical relevance) by using inclusive rather than restrictive lexical selection criteria.

---

### 379. Church (2016)

**Citation:** Church, A. T. (2016). Personality traits across cultures. *Current Opinion in Psychology, 8*, 22–30. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.copsyc.2015.09.014

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality traits, culture, universality, trait structure, cross-cultural
- *Additional terms:* lexical models, social-relational dimensions, response styles, measurement invariance, ecological context

#### Core Themes
- Reviews universality of trait structure, cultural trait-level differences, cross-cultural consistency/validity, and evolutionary/ecological contexts
- Two or three broad lexical dimensions replicate better across cultures than five- or six-factor models
- Indigenous social-relational dimensions (e.g., Interpersonal Relatedness in Chinese) may extend beyond FFM

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Efficient summary of the cross-cultural personality structure literature with useful hierarchy of lexical models table.

---

### 380. McCrae & John (1992)

**Citation:** McCrae, R. R., & John, O. P. (1992). An introduction to the five-factor model and its applications. *Journal of Personality, 60*(2), 175–215. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6494.1992.tb00970.x

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review
**Instruments:** NEO-PI-R, adjective rating scales
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* five-factor model, Big Five, personality structure, personality assessment
- *Additional terms:* factor naming, convergent validity, cross-cultural, longitudinal stability, clinical applications

#### Core Themes
- Comprehensive introduction to the FFM: history from Tupes & Christal through the 1990s consensus
- Evidence from natural language adjectives and questionnaires, convergent/discriminant validity across instruments and observers
- Argues FFM provides a common language for personality research and a framework for comprehensive assessment

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
One of the most cited personality papers; foundational reference for the five-factor framework that the project builds upon and extends.

---

### 381. De Raad & Barelds (2020)

**Citation:** De Raad, B., & Barelds, D. P. H. (2020). Models of personality structure. In P. J. Corr & G. Matthews (Eds.), *The Cambridge Handbook of Personality Psychology* (2nd ed., pp. 115–128). Cambridge University Press. https://doi.org/10.1017/9781108264822.012

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality structure, models, Big Five, lexical approach, factor analysis
- *Additional terms:* Cattell 16PF, circumplex, HEXACO, cross-cultural replication, structural models

#### Core Themes
- Traces structural models from Cattell through Big Five to HEXACO and beyond
- Discusses the lexical approach, circumplex models, and criteria for evaluating structural models
- Big Five remains the dominant paradigm but alternatives (HEXACO, Big Seven) challenge its comprehensiveness

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Useful handbook chapter providing historical context for the structural models the project evaluates.

### 382. Furnham, Crump, & Whelan (1997)

**Citation:** Furnham, A., Crump, J., & Whelan, J. (1997). Validating the NEO Personality Inventory using assessor's ratings. *Personality and Individual Differences, 22*(5), 669–675. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0191-8869(96)00261-9

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Sectional, Correlation
**Instruments:** NEO-PI, 10 management capability rating dimensions
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* NEO-PI, validation, management capability, assessor ratings, occupational behavior
- *Additional terms:* Conscientiousness, Extraversion, concurrent validity, criterion contamination

#### Core Themes
- NEO-PI five factors and subscales correlated with consultant ratings of management capability in 160 managers
- Conscientiousness and Extraversion showed strongest and most frequent correlations with management ratings; Agreeableness showed least
- Some criterion contamination: raters saw a subset of NEO scores as part of their rating algorithm

**Relevance to Book Project:** LOW
Peripheral to the main structural and developmental questions; primarily relevant for occupational validity of NEO-PI facets.

---

### 383. McCrae & Costa (1987)

**Citation:** McCrae, R. R., & Costa, P. T., Jr. (1987). Validation of the five-factor model of personality across instruments and observers. *Journal of Personality and Social Psychology, 52*(1), 81–90. https://doi.org/10.1037/0022-3514.52.1.81

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis, Multi-Method (self-reports + peer ratings)
**Instruments:** Adjective rating scales, NEO Personality Inventory
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* five-factor model, peer ratings, self-reports, cross-observer agreement, adjective factors
- *Additional terms:* convergent validity, discriminant validity, NEO Inventory, personality assessment

#### Core Themes
- Five-factor model recovered from both self-reports and peer ratings (N=275 subjects, 738 peer ratings)
- Cross-observer agreement: intraclass correlations .30–.65; peer-self correlations .25–.62 across all five factors
- Establishes that FFM is not merely a cognitive artifact but reflects observable psychological attributes

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Landmark validation study establishing multi-method convergence of the FFM; key argument for veridicality of personality traits.

---

### 384. McCrae & Costa (1997)

**Citation:** McCrae, R. R., & Costa, P. T., Jr. (1997). Personality trait structure as a human universal. *American Psychologist, 52*(5), 509–516. https://doi.org/10.1037/0003-066X.52.5.509

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis (varimax and targeted rotation), Cross-Cultural
**Instruments:** NEO-PI-R (German, Portuguese, Hebrew, Chinese, Korean, Japanese translations)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality structure, cross-cultural, universality, five-factor model, NEO-PI-R
- *Additional terms:* language families, targeted rotation, secondary loadings, cultural differences

#### Core Themes
- NEO-PI-R 30-facet structure closely replicated across six cultures spanning five language families (N=7,134)
- Targeted rotations reproduce American structure including secondary loadings
- Provides strong evidence that personality trait structure is universal rather than culturally constructed

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Key evidence for FFM universality at the facet level; complements lexical studies that find fewer universal dimensions.

---

### 385. McCrae & Costa (1999)

**Citation:** McCrae, R. R., & Costa, P. T., Jr. (1999). A five-factor theory of personality. In L. A. Pervin & O. P. John (Eds.), *Handbook of Personality: Theory and Research* (2nd ed., pp. 139–153). Guilford Press.

**Purpose:** Theory
**Methodology:** Theoretical Framework
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Five-Factor Theory, basic tendencies, characteristic adaptations, personality system
- *Additional terms:* biological bases, external influences, objective biography, self-concept

#### Core Themes
- Distinguishes basic tendencies (biologically based, endogenous traits) from characteristic adaptations (culturally conditioned attitudes, habits, skills)
- Presents FFT postulates covering origin (biological), development (intrinsic maturation), and structure (five factors)
- Argues personality traits are not mere descriptions but causal, biological entities

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Foundational theoretical framework distinguishing trait structure from its cultural expression; essential context for the biology-vs-culture questions the project addresses.

---

### 386. McCrae, Costa, Martin, et al. (2004)

**Citation:** McCrae, R. R., Costa, P. T., Jr., Martin, T. A., Oryol, V. E., Rukavishnikov, A. A., Senin, I. G., Hřebíčková, M., & Urbánek, T. (2004). Consensual validation of personality traits across cultures. *Journal of Research in Personality, 38*(2), 179–201. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0092-6566(03)00056-4

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-Cultural, Multi-Method (self/other agreement)
**Instruments:** NEO-PI-R (Russian and Czech translations)
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain, Personality Facet

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* cross-cultural, personality traits, cross-observer agreement, five-factor model
- *Additional terms:* individualism-collectivism, Russia, Czech Republic, consensual validation, gender differences

#### Core Themes
- Russian and Czech NEO-PI-R replicate American factor structure and show moderate-to-high cross-observer agreement
- Despite lower individualism scores (Russia=39, Czech Republic=58 vs. US=91), trait validity is maintained
- Counters the claim that personality traits are meaningful only in individualistic Western cultures

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Extends FFM cross-cultural evidence to Slavic/formerly Communist societies, strengthening universality claims.

---

### 387. Simms (2007)

**Citation:** Simms, L. J. (2007). The Big Seven model of personality and its relevance to personality pathology. *Journal of Personality, 75*(1), 65–94. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-6494.2006.00433.x

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Factor Analysis, Hierarchical Multiple Regression
**Instruments:** Big Seven measure, Big Five markers, personality pathology scales
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Seven, Positive Valence, Negative Valence, personality pathology, personality disorder
- *Additional terms:* incremental validity, maladaptive personality, narcissism, borderline, Big Five

#### Core Themes
- PV and NV incrementally predict personality pathology dimensions beyond Big Five (N=403 undergraduates)
- Factor analyses suggest PV may be a maladaptive aspect of Positive Emotionality and NV may be low Agreeableness rather than independent dimensions
- PV especially relevant for Narcissistic PD; NV for multiple PDs with negative self-evaluation

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Tests whether personality structure needs to extend beyond the Big Five to capture personality pathology; relevant to the evaluative dimension debate.

---

### 388. Simms, Yufik, & Gros (2010)

**Citation:** Simms, L. J., Yufik, T., & Gros, D. F. (2010). Incremental validity of Positive and Negative Valence in predicting personality disorder. *Personality Disorders: Theory, Research, and Treatment, 1*(2), 77–86. https://doi.org/10.1037/a0019752

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Hierarchical Multiple Regression
**Instruments:** Three markers each of PV, NV, and Big Five; personality disorder scales
**Level of Detail:** Personality Domain

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* Big Seven, Positive Valence, Negative Valence, personality disorder, incremental validity
- *Additional terms:* Narcissistic PD, Histrionic PD, multiple markers, dimensional models

#### Core Themes
- Replication and extension using three markers per construct (N=338): PV incrementally predicts Narcissistic and Histrionic PDs above Big Five
- NV non-specifically increments prediction of most PDs
- Multiple markers per construct strengthen confidence in evaluative dimension findings vs. Simms (2007)

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Stronger methodological test of evaluative personality dimensions' clinical relevance; supports the need for personality models broader than the Big Five for psychopathology.

---

### 389. Zeigler-Hill & Shackelford (Eds.) (2020) — Encyclopedia

**Citation:** Zeigler-Hill, V., & Shackelford, T. K. (Eds.). (2020). *Encyclopedia of Personality and Individual Differences*. Springer. https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-319-24612-3

**Purpose:** Reference Work
**Methodology:** Encyclopedia (multi-author)
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Comprehensive (all topics in personality and individual differences)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* personality, individual differences, encyclopedia, reference
- *Additional terms:* traits, assessment, development, psychopathology, biological bases, culture

#### Core Themes
- Comprehensive reference encyclopedia covering ~3,600 entries across the full landscape of personality and individual differences research
- Includes substantive entries on traits, constructs, measures, theories, biographical profiles, and methodological topics
- 5,925 pages; serves as a definitive reference for definitions and brief reviews of key constructs

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Comprehensive reference resource for construct definitions, measurement overviews, and biographical context across the entire personality domain.


#### Topical Coverage (~3,600 entries organized alphabetically)

The encyclopedia's entries span the following major thematic areas:

1. **Trait models & personality structure** (~80 entries) — Big Five, HEXACO, Big Seven, circumplex models, lexical approach, factor structure, hierarchical models, Eysenck's PEN, Cattell's 16PF, trait taxonomies
2. **Specific personality traits & temperament** (~400 entries) — Extraversion, Neuroticism, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Openness, Honesty-Humility, sensation-seeking, impulsivity, shyness, assertiveness, narcissism, empathy, grit, resilience, hostility, dominance, optimism, perfectionism, self-esteem, self-control, curiosity, creativity, etc.
3. **Emotions, affect, & mood** (~150 entries) — Positive/negative affect, emotion regulation, emotional intelligence, mood disorders, affective styles, anger, anxiety, shame, guilt, contempt, jealousy, gratitude, awe
4. **Motivation, goals, & values** (~120 entries) — Achievement motivation, intrinsic/extrinsic motivation, self-determination theory, goal-setting, values, need for cognition, need for closure, need for belonging
5. **Self & identity** (~150 entries) — Self-concept, self-esteem, self-efficacy, self-regulation, self-compassion, identity development, possible selves, self-discrepancy theory, self-enhancement, narcissism
6. **Personality assessment & psychometrics** (~215 entries) — Named scales and inventories (NEO-PI-R, BFI, HEXACO-PI-R, MMPI, 16PF, PANAS, CPI, PAI, etc.), response styles, acquiescence, measurement invariance, reliability, validity
7. **Biological bases: genetics, neuroscience, & evolution** (~180 entries) — Behavioral genetics, heritability, twin studies, molecular genetics, neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine), brain structures (amygdala, prefrontal cortex, BIS/BAS), evolutionary psychology, life history theory, hormones (testosterone, cortisol, oxytocin)
8. **Personality development & aging** (~100 entries) — Maturity principle, rank-order stability, mean-level change, temperament in childhood, adolescent development, midlife, aging, identity development stages
9. **Personality disorders & psychopathology** (~80 entries) — DSM personality disorders (narcissistic, borderline, antisocial, avoidant, schizotypal, etc.), Dark Triad/Tetrad, dimensional models of pathology, comorbidity
10. **Cognitive styles & intelligence** (~90 entries) — Cognitive ability, creativity, wisdom, need for cognition, field dependence, attributional style, locus of control, mindfulness, absorption, openness to experience
11. **Social & interpersonal processes** (~200 entries) — Attachment theory, interpersonal circumplex, social dominance, aggression, prosocial behavior, cooperation, trust, forgiveness, loneliness, rejection, social skills
12. **Culture, gender, & group differences** (~80 entries) — Cross-cultural personality, individualism-collectivism, gender differences, racial/ethnic identity, acculturation, indigenous personality models
13. **Clinical & therapeutic approaches** (~120 entries) — Psychoanalytic theory, CBT, psychotherapy process, defense mechanisms, transference, therapeutic alliance, clinical assessment, treatment outcomes
14. **Health, well-being, & coping** (~120 entries) — Subjective well-being, life satisfaction, coping strategies, stress, Type A/D personality, health behavior, post-traumatic growth, meaning in life
15. **Work, leadership, & organizational psychology** (~80 entries) — Personality and job performance, leadership, vocational interests, Holland types, organizational citizenship, burnout, career development
16. **Psychoanalytic & historical theories** (~100 entries) — Freud, Jung, Adler, Erikson, object relations, ego psychology, psychosexual stages, defense mechanisms, unconscious processes
17. **Sexuality & relationships** (~60 entries) — Mate preferences, romantic attachment, sexual orientation, relationship satisfaction, infidelity, love styles, sexual personality
18. **Morality, religion, & spirituality** (~50 entries) — Moral development, moral foundations, religiosity, spiritual transcendence, moral identity, virtues, character strengths
19. **Methodology & statistics** (~60 entries) — Factor analysis, SEM, multilevel modeling, experience sampling, longitudinal methods, measurement equivalence, effect sizes, replication
20. **Biographical entries** (~310 entries) — Profiles of major researchers (e.g., Allport, Cattell, Eysenck, Costa, McCrae, Goldberg, Tellegen, Mischel, Bandura, Bowlby, Ainsworth, Murray, and hundreds of contemporary personality scientists)

**Total:** ~3,600 entries across 5,925 pages.

---

### 390. Sánchez-Villagra et al. (2017)

**Citation:** Sánchez-Villagra, M. R., Segura, V., Geiger, M., Heck, L., Veitschegger, K., & Flores, D. (2017). On the lack of a universal pattern associated with mammalian domestication: differences in skull growth trajectories across phylogeny. *Royal Society Open Science*, *4*, 170876. https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.170876

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Multivariate (PCA + jackknife resampling), Bivariate (SMA regression), Cross-Species Comparative
**Instruments:** 14 linear skull measurements; 13 wild–domesticated mammal pairs; N = 1,128 museum specimens
**Level of Detail:** Other (developmental morphology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* ontogeny, development, dog, cat, horse, modularity, heterochrony, domestication, paedomorphosis
- *Additional terms:* skull growth trajectories, allometry, isometry, hypomorphosis, hypermorphosis, peramorphosis, domestication syndrome, allometric growth, developmental penetrance, neural crest

#### Core Themes
- No universal heterochronic pattern (e.g., paedomorphosis or peramorphosis) underlies mammalian domestication across the 13 species pairs studied
- Dogs and llamas diverge most from their wild counterparts in skull growth trajectories; pigs, camels, and goats diverge least
- Wild forms exhibit more isometric growth; domesticated forms tend toward more allometric growth, which correlates with their capacity for morphological diversification under selective breeding
- Carnivorous domesticates (dogs, ferrets, cats) show hypomorphosis (shortened trajectories); herbivorous domesticates (horse, sheep, goat, rabbit) show hypermorphosis (extended trajectories)
- Skull modularity means that even when heterochrony occurs, its effects are not global but region-specific within the skull
- Domestication effects on prenatal growth are hypothesized but untested, suggesting "developmental penetrance" as a complementary mechanism

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Directly addresses the developmental origins of phenotypic diversity via domestication — a paradigm case of how selection reshapes developmental trajectories to produce new phenotypes. Relevant to the book's themes of how phenotypes are made via development and how human intervention (domestication as a form of artificial selection) changes the developmental rules governing phenotypic outcomes.

---

### 391. Sih, Bell, & Johnson (2004)

**Citation:** Sih, A., Bell, A., & Johnson, J. C. (2004). Behavioral syndromes: an ecological and evolutionary overview. *Trends in Ecology and Evolution*, *19*(7), 372–378. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tree.2004.04.009

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Other (behavioral ecology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* behavioral syndrome, behavioral type, individual differences, bold/shy axis, aggression, proactive/reactive
- *Additional terms:* animal personality, behavioral correlation across situations, limited behavioral plasticity, ecological trap, invasive species, coping styles, reaction norm

#### Core Themes
- Introduces behavioral syndromes (suites of correlated behaviors across multiple contexts) as the animal-behavior ecology analog of human personality
- Key shift: from studying optimal plasticity per isolated situation to studying correlated behavioral constraints across situations; limited plasticity can explain apparently non-adaptive behavior (e.g., excess sexual cannibalism, inappropriate boldness near predators)
- Three main syndrome types reviewed: activity syndromes (feeding vs. predator avoidance tradeoffs), aggressiveness syndromes, and proactive/reactive (bold/shy) axis
- Behavioral syndromes explain maintenance of individual variation, influence species distributions, and predict responses to environmental change; bold/invasive species connection
- Great tit work (Dingemanse, Drent, van Oers) demonstrated heritability and fitness consequences of exploration/coping style; fitness consequences depend on competitive conditions (fast explorers benefit in intense competition, slow in relaxed)
- Behavioral syndromes as evolutionary constraints (Box 2) vs. adaptive correlations — populations and species can evolve different syndrome structures

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Foundational paper showing that animal personality/individual differences are not just a curiosity but have major ecological and evolutionary consequences. Directly connects the animal behavior literature to human personality psychology and supports the book's thesis that personality-like phenotypes are deeply conserved and functionally important across taxa.

---

### 392. Sih, Bell, Johnson, & Ziemba (2004)

**Citation:** Sih, A., Bell, A. M., Johnson, J. C., & Ziemba, R. E. (2004). Behavioral syndromes: an integrative overview. *Quarterly Review of Biology*, *79*(3), 241–277. https://doi.org/10.1086/422893

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review, Theoretical
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Other (behavioral ecology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* behavioral syndrome, animal personality, coping styles, bold/shy, proactive/reactive, behavioral correlation
- *Additional terms:* Big Five personality, reaction norm, behavioral type, neuroendocrine basis, QTL, repeatability, gene × environment, frequency-dependent selection

#### Core Themes
- Comprehensive treatment of the behavioral syndromes concept: defines terminology, distinguishes behavioral type (individual-level) from behavioral syndrome (population-level correlated structure)
- Reviews measurement methods: repeatability coefficients, behavioral reaction norms, cross-context correlations; addresses design challenges
- Covers proximate mechanisms: genetic basis (QTL, heritability in great tits, mice, Drosophila), neuroendocrine mechanisms (HPA axis, testosterone, coping styles), effects of early experience and developmental environment
- Explicitly maps animal behavioral syndromes to human Big Five personality framework; bold/shy ↔ Extraversion/Openness; proactive/reactive ↔ combinations across domains
- Evolutionary perspectives: frequency-dependent selection maintaining variation; genetic correlations as constraint vs. adaptive integration; pace-of-life considerations
- Ecological implications: species invasions, species distributions, human-disturbed habitats, and the importance of within-species variation in behavioral type

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
The most comprehensive single treatment of behavioral syndromes and animal personality, including explicit connections to human personality (Big Five). Essential for the book's argument that personality-like phenotypes are universal features of behavioral systems made by the same evolutionary and developmental processes.

---

### 393. Sih, Ferrari, & Harris (2011)

**Citation:** Sih, A., Ferrari, M. C. O., & Harris, D. J. (2011). Evolution and behavioural responses to human-induced rapid environmental change. *Evolutionary Applications*, *4*(2), 367–387. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1752-4571.2010.00166.x

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review, Theoretical (detection-theory/sensory ecology framework)
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Other (behavioral ecology / evolutionary biology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* HIREC, human-induced rapid environmental change, behavioral mismatch, evolutionary mismatch, ecological trap, sensory ecology, detection theory
- *Additional terms:* discriminability, response bias, cue reliability, signal detection theory, behavioral plasticity, cue similarity, evolutionary trap, learning constraints, behavioral syndrome

#### Core Themes
- Proposes a sensory-ecology / signal detection framework for predicting when animals will respond appropriately vs. maladaptively (evolutionary mismatches) to HIREC
- Key variables: discriminability (how well animals can distinguish new cues from ancestral ones), response bias (innate tendency toward particular responses), cue reliability, and plasticity costs
- Distinguishes behavioral plasticity responses (within-lifetime adjustment) from evolutionary change (across generations); identifies conditions favoring each
- Behavioral syndromes (limited behavioral plasticity) are a major constraint on adaptive responses to HIREC; proactive individuals especially prone to maladaptive boldness in novel dangerous contexts
- Develops specific predictions: mismatches are worse when novel cues are similar to ancestral cues with opposite valence (ecological traps), when sensory systems are tuned away from novel cue ranges, and when learning is slow relative to environmental change rate
- Integrates predator–prey, sexual selection, and habitat selection contexts as three domains where HIREC creates behavioral mismatches

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE–HIGH
Directly relevant to how behavior evolved to respond to environmental signals — connects behavioral phenotype design (via evolution) to modern environments. Relevant to the book's theme of evolutionary mismatch and how phenotypes shaped in ancestral environments may function poorly in novel ones.

---

### 394. Sih, Cote, Evans, Fogarty, & Pruitt (2012)

**Citation:** Sih, A., Cote, J., Evans, M., Fogarty, S., & Pruitt, J. (2012). Ecological implications of behavioural syndromes. *Ecology Letters*, *15*(3), 278–289. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1461-0248.2011.01731.x

**Purpose:** Review
**Methodology:** Narrative Review, Theoretical
**Instruments:** N/A
**Level of Detail:** Other (behavioral ecology / community ecology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* behavioral syndrome, animal personality, pace-of-life syndrome, ecological implications, species interactions, population dynamics
- *Additional terms:* among-individual variation, behavioral type, trophic cascade, invasion ecology, HIREC, social network, personality-dependent dispersal, extended phenotype

#### Core Themes
- Organizes ecological consequences of behavioral syndromes into three tiers: (1) species-mean behavioral type affecting distributions and interactions, (2) within-species variation in behavioral type affecting population dynamics and resilience, (3) behavioral correlations (syndromes) affecting community processes
- Average behavioral type determines species distributions (habitat filtering), interspecific interactions (predation rates, competition outcomes), and population dynamics; bold species interact differently with predators, competitors, and prey
- Within-species variation in behavioral type buffers populations against environmental perturbations; aggressive personality types can drive overexploitation in some communities; more variation = more resilience
- Behavioral correlations (syndromes) create non-obvious cross-context effects: dispersal syndromes can link invasion history to competitive outcomes; reproductive personality types affect offspring behavioral phenotypes
- Introduces connection to pace-of-life syndromes: bold/active behavioral types tend to correlate with fast life histories (high fecundity, early reproduction, short lifespan)
- HIREC section: within-species variation in behavioral type determines whether populations contain individuals that can behaviorally match changed conditions

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Extends behavioral syndrome theory into community ecology and conservation; relevant background for understanding how individual-level behavioral phenotypes scale up to population and community outcomes. Less directly relevant to the book's core focus on how mental phenotypes are made, but important for ecological consequences.

---

### 395. Singh et al. (2025)

**Citation:** Singh, A., et al. (2025). *State or personality trait: Determinants of boldness and shoal size preference in adult zebrafish*. bioRxiv. https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.02.12.637681

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Repeated-measures behavioral experiment; within-subject design across hunger states and test cycles
**Instruments:** Open Field Test (OFT), Emergence Test (ET), Social Preference Test (binary 4v2 and ternary 4v2v1 shoal choice)
**Level of Detail:** Other (behavioral ecology; individual variation)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* boldness, personality, state, shoal size preference, zebrafish, hunger, Open Field Test, repeatability
- *Additional terms:* within-individual consistency, behavioral type, animal personality, state-dependent behavior, repeatability coefficient, intraclass correlation

#### Core Themes
- Tests whether OFT exploration, emergence latency, and shoal size preference qualify as stable personality traits (vs. hunger-modulated states) in adult male zebrafish
- Only OFT area exploration shows significant repeatability (R ≈ 0.36); emergence latency (ET) is reduced by hunger but lacks stable individual consistency; shoal size preference is not repeatable
- Dissociates "state" effects (hunger modulates emergence latency) from "trait" effects (OFT exploration is hunger-insensitive and individually consistent)
- Small N (24 males) limits statistical power; results should be treated as preliminary/exploratory
- Highlights the importance of testing multiple behavioral measures and multiple motivational states before concluding a behavior represents a "personality trait"

**Relevance to Book Project:** MODERATE
Methodologically illustrates the state/trait distinction in animal personality research; directly relevant to the measurement challenge of distinguishing stable phenotypes from situationally modulated states. Relevant to the book's epistemological themes about how mental phenotypes are identified and measured by psychological science. Treat with caution: preprint, small N.

---

### 396. Sol, Bacher, Reader, & Lefebvre (2008)

**Citation:** Sol, D., Bacher, S., Reader, S. M., & Lefebvre, L. (2008). Brain size predicts the success of mammal species introduced into novel environments. *American Naturalist*, *172*(S1), S63–S71. https://doi.org/10.1086/588304

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Generalized Linear Mixed Models (GLIMMIX); comparative phylogenetically-controlled cross-species analysis; global introduction database
**Instruments:** Residual brain mass (taxonomically-adjusted and phylogenetically independent contrast methods); N = 443 introduction events, 66 species with brain data
**Level of Detail:** Other (comparative neuroscience / ecology)

**Key Terms:**
- *Paper keywords:* brain size, invasion biology, behavioral flexibility, propagule pressure, ecological generalism, establishment success
- *Additional terms:* cognitive buffer hypothesis, brain size–environmental change hypothesis, residual brain size, behavioral innovation, allometric grade shifts, phylogenetic autocorrelation

#### Core Themes
- First empirical test of the brain size–environmental change hypothesis in mammals: relative brain size predicts establishment success in novel environments after controlling for introduction effort, habitat generalism, phylogeny, and biogeographic region
- Key distinction: absolute brain size and body mass are not predictive; only residual brain size (allometrically corrected) predicts success
- Replicates and extends equivalent findings in birds (Sol et al. 2005 PNAS) to the largest-brained vertebrate class, increasing cross-taxonomic generality
- Effect is robust to species with single introduction events and to removal of the most frequently introduced genus (*Cervus*)
- Interprets results via behavioral flexibility (learning, innovation, decision-making) as proximate cognitive mechanisms enabling survival of novel ecological challenges
- Connects to cognitive buffer hypothesis: enlarged brain buffers individuals against vagaries of novel environments; reduces mortality; facilitates population persistence

**Relevance to Book Project:** HIGH
Central to the book's argument about how brain evolution tracks the need for behavioral flexibility; provides empirical foundation for the cognitive buffer / brain size–behavioral flexibility nexus. Directly addresses how cognitive phenotypes (indexed by brain size) were made by natural selection operating on ecological challenges.

---

### 397. Sol, Olkowicz, Sayol, et al. (2022)

**Citation:** Sol, D., Olkowicz, S., Sayol, F., Swanson, E. M., Bertolino, S., Teeling, E. C., Mazzoldi, C., Łukasik, P., Jiang, Z., Sheratt, T. N., Morand-Ferron, J., & Lefebvre, L. (2022). Neuron numbers link innovativeness with both absolute and relative brain size in birds. *Nature Ecology & Evolution*, *6*, 1381–1389. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41559-022-01815-x

**Purpose:** Original Research
**Methodology:** Cross-species comparative; isotropic fractionator for neuron counting; Bayesian phylogenetic mixed models; random forests; innovation frequency database
**Instruments:** Isotropic fractionator; innovation frequency database (>4,400 published reports); Bayesian phylogenetic models (brms); N = 111 bird species
